title: Lawmakers reintroduce cannabis banking reform

summary_ai.digest: Sen. Jeff Merkley reintroduced cannabis banking reform legislation following the DEA's rescheduling of medical cannabis, opening a regulatory window for banks to serve the cannabis industry.
summary_ai.summary_long: The reintroduction of cannabis banking reform comes on the heels of the DEA's decision to reschedule cannabis from Schedule I to Schedule III, which removes a key federal barrier to bank participation in the sector. Previously, the Controlled Substances Act prevented most federally insured banks from servicing cannabis businesses, forcing the industry into cash-based operations. The rescheduling creates both a regulatory trigger and political momentum for legislative action. If passed, banking access would unlock working capital, payment processing, and lending products for an industry currently trapped in high-cost alternative finance. This directly expands the addressable market for regional and community banks, particularly those with retail and small-business relationships.
talking_points: DEA rescheduling of medical cannabis to Schedule III removes the federal statute blocking bank participation—creates a legislative window for real banking access, not just guidance.

Timing matters: political momentum + regulatory change = higher passage probability than prior attempts; watch for companion House bill and committee sponsor signals.
summary_ai.highlight: DEA rescheduling of medical cannabis to Schedule III removes the federal statutory barrier that has blocked bank participation for a decade—this is the regulatory catalyst needed to unlock legislation.

The piece argues this structural shift will reshape estate planning, reduce the pool of capital flowing to heirs, and likely depress demand for trust and wealth management services built on inheritance velocity. Healthcare and long-term care costs are the largest uninsured expense for high-net-worth retirees; a decade of added lifespan in a skilled facility can consume $500K–$1M+ of investable assets.

Wealth management and RIA platforms are structurally exposed to this shift—their fee revenue and M&A multiples rest partly on recurring inherited AUM; slower intergenerational transfers compress growth assumptions.
summary_ai.highlight: If the $84 trillion forecast moderates even 25% due to longevity spend-down, inherited AUM flowing to RIA platforms and wealth managers shrinks by trillions, reshaping consolidation economics.
